text: Abram went up from Egypt to the Negev with his wife and everything he owned, and Lot went with him.
Abram had become very rich in livestock, silver, and gold.
He traveled in stages from the Negev back to Bethel, to the place between Bethel and Ai where his tent had been before.
He returned to the site of the altar he had built earlier and called on the LORD's name there.
Lot, who traveled with Abram, also had flocks, herds, and tents.
The land could not support them living together, since their possessions were too great.
Quarrels broke out between Abram's herdsmen and Lot's herdsmen; the Canaanites and Perizzites were also living in the land.
Abram said to Lot, let there be no strife between us or our herdsmen, since we are relatives.
Abram said the whole land was open to Lot: if he went left, Abram would go right, and vice versa.
Lot looked out and saw the whole Jordan plain was well watered, like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, toward Zoar.
So Lot chose the whole Jordan plain for himself and journeyed east; the two men parted from each other.
Abram stayed in Canaan while Lot settled among the cities of the plain, pitching his tents near Sodom.
The men of Sodom were wicked, great sinners against the LORD.
After Lot left, the LORD told Abram to look in every direction from where he stood.
God said all the land Abram could see he would give to him and his offspring forever.
God said he would make Abram's offspring like the dust of the earth, too many to count.
God told Abram to walk through the length and breadth of the land, for he was giving it to him.
So Abram moved his tents and settled near the oaks of Mamre at Hebron, and built an altar to the LORD there.
